threats.title: transferFrom Fee Deduction Violates ERC20 Allowance Invariant, Extracting More Than Approved From Sender
threats.hypothesis_description: _payTxFee deducts transaction fees from the sender via _update() before super.transferFrom() is called. The ERC20 allowance check in super.transferFrom only covers the transfer amount, not the additional fee. A spender can therefore cause the sender to lose amount + fee while only needing amount in allowance. At 100% fee rate (10000/10000), a 100 EURF allowance allows extracting 200 EURF from the sender. This breaks all composability with protocols relying on ERC20 allowance semantics.
threats.investigation_rationale: The fee is deducted via a raw _update() call outside the ERC20 allowance accounting path, meaning the allowance check in transferFrom never accounts for the fee amount. Any spender with a valid allowance can trigger this on-chain with no special conditions beyond a non-zero fee rate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _payTxFee calls _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) before super.transferFrom; fee deduction is outside allowance accounting

preconditions: _txfeeRate > 0
_feesFaucet != address(0)
Sender has granted non-zero allowance to spender
Sender has balance >= amount + fee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) at Token.sol:133 executes before super.transferFrom at Token.sol:140, bypassing allowance check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Fee rate set by ADMIN; any non-zero rate triggers the vulnerability

operational_questions: What is the deployed _txfeeRate value?
Do any DeFi integrations rely on standard ERC20 allowance semantics for EURF?
Can a spender systematically drain sender balance beyond allowance?
validation_requirements: PoC: Approve spender for X, call transferFrom for X, verify sender loses X + fee
Test with 100% fee rate to maximize impact
Verify no allowance reduction for the fee portion

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain valid allowance from a sender (normal DeFi operation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call transferFrom for the full approved amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Sender's balance is reduced by amount + fee; only amount was approved
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: At high fee rates, the excess deduction is significant
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - any spender with allowance can execute

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All DeFi integrations relying on allowance semantics are affected; DEXs, lending protocols, aggregators will malfunction
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix fee accounting
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All transferFrom calls when fees are active

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor transferFrom calls where sender balance decrease > allowance consumed
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Spenders calling transferFrom for maximum allowance amounts

threats.title: Forwarder Accepts Arbitrary Domain Separators Enabling Cross-Chain/Cross-Contract Signature Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: The Forwarder's _verifySig() accepts the domainSeparator as a caller-supplied parameter without validating it against the contract's own EIP-712 domain. An attacker who observes a meta-transaction executed on another chain (or another Forwarder instance) can replay that signature by providing the original domain separator. If the user has the same nonce on both chains and the EURF token address matches, the transfer executes without the user's consent on the target chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: The domainSeparator parameter flows directly from external input into the digest computation at Forwarder.sol:_verifySig without any validation. EIP-712 mandates domain separation to prevent exactly this class of replay attacks.
deployment_assumptions: Forwarder deployed on multiple chains with matching EURF token addresses
User nonces start at 0 on each chain independently
threats.config_evidence: Documentation states target chains are Ethereum and Polygon; hardhat.config.js shows sepolia and amoy (Polygon) network configs

proof_of_signal.pattern: domainSeparator parameter used directly in keccak256(abi.encodePacked('\x19\x01', domainSeparator, ...)) without validation against contract's own domain

preconditions: User executed a meta-tx on chain A (publicly visible after confirmation)
Same user has matching nonce on chain B's Forwarder
req.to matches _eurfAddress on chain B
Same ForwardRequest type hash registered on both Forwarders

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: domainSeparator is never compared to any contract-derived value; typeHashes check is necessary but insufficient for domain binding
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any relayer/attacker can supply arbitrary domainSeparator
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for any user who has used meta-tx on one chain and has unused nonces on another

operational_questions: Are Forwarder and EURF deployed at identical addresses on Ethereum and Polygon?
How many users have matching nonces across chains?
Can the attack extend to same-chain different-Forwarder-instance replay?
validation_requirements: Deploy on two testnets, execute meta-tx on chain A, replay on chain B with chain A's domainSeparator
Verify ECDSA.recover succeeds with foreign domainSeparator
Confirm nonce consumption and token transfer on chain B

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor chain A for meta-transactions via Forwarder, extract ForwardRequest params and signature from calldata
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Check user's nonce on chain B's Forwarder (public view function)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit execute() on chain B with chain A's domainSeparator and the extracted signature
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User's tokens on chain B are transferred without consent
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single attacker, publicly available data

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose tokens on secondary chains without signing any authorization for that chain
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Irreversible token transfer; requires admin forceTransfer to remediate
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All meta-transaction users on multi-chain deployments

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single transaction using publicly observed data
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - extract calldata from block explorer, construct replay tx
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After user's first meta-tx on any chain, before they transact on the target chain

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: User tokens moved without corresponding user-initiated action

threats.title: Revoked Controller Can Re-Enable Operations After Role Removal, Bypassing Safety Switch
threats.hypothesis_description: When a controller activates the safety switch, _operatingController is set to their address. The re-enable path checks _operatingController == _msgSender() but does NOT verify the caller still holds the CONTROLLER role. A controller whose role is revoked (e.g., due to compromise) retains the ability to call safetySwitch() and re-enable operations, allowing minting and burning to resume against the admin's wishes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The re-enable code path at line 72-75 explicitly allows _operatingController to re-enable regardless of current role membership. This is a direct authorization bypass that persists even after role revocation.
preconditions: A CONTROLLER previously activated safetySwitch (setting _operatingController to their address)
Owner revoked the CONTROLLER role from that address
_operating is currently false

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: safetySwitch re-enable only checks address match, not role membership
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner's removeController cannot fully revoke the controller's re-enable authority
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Indefinite - persists until _operating is re-enabled by admin or another mechanism

operational_questions: Can the admin work around this by calling safetySwitch themselves first?
Is there a race condition where the revoked controller re-enables before admin can respond?
validation_requirements: PoC: Controller activates switch, owner removes role, former controller calls safetySwitch again
Verify operations resume after revoked controller re-enables

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Controller activates safetySwitch (legitimate or part of attack)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner detects compromise and removes CONTROLLER role
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Revoked controller calls safetySwitch() - passes _operatingController check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Operations resume, enabling minting/burning by any active minter
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None after initial controller role assignment

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Safety mechanism becomes unreliable during incident response
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Minting/burning resumes during active security incident
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin must pause() the contract as alternative; safetySwitch alone insufficient

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After role revocation, before admin can pause

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: safetySwitch called by address without CONTROLLER role
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Operating state toggled unexpectedly after controller removal
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Former controller address making transactions post-revocation

threats.title: Mint and Burn Operations Bypass Blacklist and Pause Checks, Breaking Compliance Controls
threats.hypothesis_description: mint() and burn() only check the _operating flag but do not call adminSanity() or check paused()/isBlacklisted(). A minter can mint tokens directly to blacklisted (sanctioned) addresses, and both mint and burn continue to function while the contract is paused. For a regulated EUR stablecoin, this creates compliance violations where sanctioned entities receive tokens and supply can be manipulated during emergency pauses. The burn() NatSpec comment claims 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but the code does not implement this check.
threats.investigation_rationale: mint() at ERC20ControlerMinterUpgradeable.sol:137-149 checks roles and _operating but never calls adminSanity, isBlacklisted, or paused(). This is a clear omission in a compliance-focused stablecoin.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint() and burn() check hasRole and _operating but never check paused() or isBlacklisted()

preconditions: Caller has MINTER_ROLE or MASTER_MINTER
_operating is true
Target address is blacklisted (for mint bypass) or contract is paused (for pause bypass)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No blacklist or pause checks in mint/burn code paths
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Minter acts within their role but bypasses compliance controls
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always present when blacklisted addresses exist or contract is paused

operational_questions: Are there off-chain controls preventing minters from minting to blacklisted addresses?
During a pause, is _operating also set to false as a secondary defense?
Does the burn NatSpec mismatch indicate a missing implementation?
validation_requirements: PoC: Blacklist an address, mint to it, verify success
PoC: Pause contract, call mint, verify success
Verify burn allows blacklisted minter to burn

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tokens created for sanctioned entity in violation of compliance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Alternatively: admin pauses for emergency, minter continues mint/burn
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires MINTER_ROLE - legitimate or compromised minter

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Compliance controls ineffective for supply operations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tokens minted to blacklisted address must be force-transferred back

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When blacklisted addresses exist or contract is paused

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Mint events where 'to' address is in blacklist

threats.title: Missing Storage Gaps in Abstract Contracts Creates Upgrade Storage Collision Risk
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable, ERC20AdminUpgradeable, ERC20ControlerMinterUpgradeable, and FeesHandlerUpgradeable are upgradeable abstract contracts that define custom state variables but lack __gap storage arrays. Only Token.sol has uint256[49] __gap. If any of these intermediate contracts add new state variables during an upgrade, the storage layout shifts, causing collision with downstream contract storage. This can corrupt balances, roles, fee configurations, or any other state, potentially leading to total loss of funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: OpenZeppelin's upgradeable contract pattern requires __gap arrays in every contract that may be upgraded. Four custom abstract contracts omit them while defining custom state variables (_nonces, _blacklist, minterAllowed, _feesFaucet, etc.).
threats.config_evidence: Deployment uses UUPS pattern via hardhat-upgrades (deployToken.js). upgradeToken.js shows upgrade flow exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Defines _nonces, _PERMIT_TYPEHASH, _TWA_TYPEHASH, _trustedForwarder private state variables but no __gap array; same pattern in all 4 abstract contracts

preconditions: An upgrade is performed that adds new state variables to any of the four abstract contracts
hardhat-upgrades safety checks are bypassed or misconfigured

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Token.sol:__gap exists (line 163) but no intermediate contracts have gaps
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: OWNER controls upgrades; storage corruption is accidental or intentional
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any future upgrade that modifies intermediate contract storage layout

operational_questions: Does hardhat-upgrades catch this during upgrade validation?
Are there planned feature additions to these abstract contracts?
What is the exact storage slot layout across all inherited contracts?
validation_requirements: Run forge inspect storage-layout on EURFToken
Attempt upgrade adding a variable to ERC20AdminUpgradeable, verify hardhat-upgrades flags it
Map exact storage slots for all state variables

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify storage layout via compilation artifacts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Plan upgrade that adds variable to an intermediate contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If safety checks are bypassed, new variable overlaps with existing storage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Storage corruption affects balances, roles, or critical configuration

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Role storage corruption could grant unintended privileges
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Balance corruption, fee configuration corruption, complete protocol malfunction
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: May require redeployment; storage corruption is difficult to recover from
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All contract functionality if storage is corrupted

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During any upgrade that modifies intermediate contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: upgradeToAndCall events followed by state corruption
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected values in state variables after upgrade

threats.title: Phantom Fee Requirement Blocks Transfers When feesFaucet Is address(0) With Non-Zero Fee Rate
threats.hypothesis_description: When _txfeeRate > 0 but _feesFaucet == address(0), _payTxFee requires balanceOf(from) >= txFees + txAmount but skips the actual fee deduction (the _update call is conditional on _feesFaucet != address(0)). Users with balance >= amount but < amount + phantom_fee cannot transfer despite no fees being collected. This effectively freezes a portion of every user's balance equal to the fee percentage.
threats.investigation_rationale: The balance check at Token.sol:133 is unconditional, but the _update at Token.sol:134 is conditional on _feesFaucet != address(0). This creates an inconsistent state where the balance requirement exceeds actual deductions.
deployment_assumptions: _txfeeRate set to non-zero value before _feesFaucet is configured
threats.config_evidence: deployToken.js does not call setFeeFaucet or setTxFeeRate; these must be set post-deployment. If setTxFeeRate is called before setFeeFaucet, the phantom fee condition activates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Balance check `balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount` is unconditional; fee transfer `_update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees)` is conditional on `_feesFaucet != address(0)`

preconditions: _txfeeRate > 0
_feesFaucet == address(0)
User has balance >= amount but < amount + calculateTxFee(amount)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unconditional balance check with conditional fee deduction
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: ADMIN sets fee rate without setting feesFaucet
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Active whenever both conditions hold simultaneously

operational_questions: Is there an operational procedure ensuring feesFaucet is set before txfeeRate?
Could feesFaucet be reset to address(0) while txfeeRate remains active?
validation_requirements: Set txfeeRate to 100 (1%), leave feesFaucet at address(0)
Attempt transfer with exact balance, verify revert
Verify no fee is actually collected

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe that _feesFaucet is address(0) and _txfeeRate is non-zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any user with balance == transfer_amount will be unable to transfer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Effective DoS on users with tight balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: N/A - systemic issue, not targeted attack
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - configuration issue affects all users

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users with exact balances cannot transfer; potential support burden
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin sets feesFaucet or resets txfeeRate to 0

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transfers with BalanceTooLow reverts when user balance appears sufficient

threats.title: MASTER_MINTER Minting Becomes Allowance-Limited If Also Granted MINTER_ROLE
threats.hypothesis_description: In mint(), the allowance deduction applies when hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) is true, regardless of whether the caller also has MASTER_MINTER. If a MASTER_MINTER is also granted MINTER_ROLE (e.g., via addMinter), their unlimited minting becomes bounded by minterAllowed[address]. This contradicts the tiered minting design where MASTER_MINTER should always have unlimited authority. A MASTER_MINTER calling addMinter(self, X) to test minter functionality would inadvertently restrict their own minting to X.
threats.investigation_rationale: mint() at line 143 checks MINTER_ROLE independently of MASTER_MINTER status. The MINTER_ROLE check and allowance deduction at lines 144-148 executes for any caller with MINTER_ROLE, even if they passed the initial MASTER_MINTER check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: First if checks MASTER_MINTER || MINTER_ROLE for access; second if at line 143 checks MINTER_ROLE alone for allowance deduction - no MASTER_MINTER exclusion

preconditions: Address holds both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE simultaneously
minterAllowed[address] < desired mint amount

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Allowance check applies to all MINTER_ROLE holders without MASTER_MINTER exclusion
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: MASTER_MINTER can grant themselves MINTER_ROLE via addMinter(self, X)

operational_questions: Can MASTER_MINTER self-remediate by calling removeMinter(self)?
Are there operational procedures preventing dual-role assignment?
Could this be exploited to DoS the MASTER_MINTER's minting capability?
validation_requirements: Grant both roles to same address, attempt mint exceeding allowance, verify revert
Verify MASTER_MINTER can remove own MINTER_ROLE to restore unlimited minting

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: MASTER_MINTER grants themselves MINTER_ROLE via addMinter(self, smallAmount)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: MASTER_MINTER's unlimited minting is effectively restricted
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: MASTER_MINTER self-inflicted or via social engineering

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: MASTER_MINTER's unlimited authority is silently restricted
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Minting operations blocked until dual-role is resolved
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: MASTER_MINTER calls removeMinter(self) to restore unlimited minting
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All mint operations by dual-role holder

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Same address holding both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE

threats.title: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init Declared Public Instead of Internal Risks State Reset During Upgrades
threats.hypothesis_description: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init is declared as `public onlyInitializing` instead of the standard `internal onlyInitializing` pattern used by OpenZeppelin. While protected by onlyInitializing at runtime, this exposes the function to external calls during any initializing context (including upgrade reinitializers). If called during an upgrade, it would re-call __ERC20_init (resetting token name/symbol), __AccessControlEnumerable_init, and grant MASTER_MINTER to address(0), overwriting the legitimate MASTER_MINTER and disrupting the entire minter hierarchy.
threats.investigation_rationale: All other init functions in the codebase use `internal onlyInitializing`. This function uniquely uses `public onlyInitializing`, deviating from the pattern. The function body resets critical state: _setRoleAdmin(MINTER_ROLE, MASTER_MINTER), _grantRole(MASTER_MINTER, address(0)), and reinitializes ERC20 and AccessControl.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function declared `public onlyInitializing` instead of `internal onlyInitializing`; calls __ERC20_init and grants MASTER_MINTER to address(0)

preconditions: Contract is in an initializing state (during upgrade with reinitializer)
External caller or malicious upgrade code invokes __ERC20ControlerMinter_init

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public visibility allows external invocation during initializing phase
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any upgrade that enters initializing state

operational_questions: Can this function be called during a reinitializer(N) context in upgrades?
Would calling it during upgrade overwrite the current MASTER_MINTER?
Does the onlyInitializing guard fully prevent post-deployment external calls?
validation_requirements: Attempt external call to __ERC20ControlerMinter_init post-deployment, verify revert
Simulate upgrade with reinitializer and attempt calling this function
Verify state reset effects if called during initialization

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for or trigger a contract upgrade
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: MASTER_MINTER reset to address(0), ERC20 state reinitialized
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Minter management disrupted; no address can manage minters
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires upgrade in progress - narrow window

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: MASTER_MINTER reset to address(0), minter hierarchy broken
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cannot add/remove minters or update allowances until MASTER_MINTER is reassigned
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: OWNER must call setMasterMinter to restore

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - requires upgrade to be in progress

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: MASTER_MINTER role granted to address(0) after deployment
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: External calls to init functions during upgrades

threats.title: setOwner Can Create Multiple OWNER Members If grantRole Is Called Directly, Enabling Hidden Privilege Persistence
threats.hypothesis_description: setOwner assumes exactly one OWNER exists (using getRoleMember(OWNER, 0) to identify the current owner). However, the inherited grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, address) is publicly accessible to any current OWNER, allowing direct creation of additional OWNER members. Once multiple OWNERs exist, setOwner's revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) removes the member at index 0 (which may not be the intended target), leaving hidden OWNER members with full upgrade authority, role management, and fund access.
threats.investigation_rationale: setOwner at Token.sol:80-84 performs grantRole then revokeRole(getRoleMember(OWNER,0)). EnumerableSet ordering means index 0 may not be the caller after multiple grants. The owner() view function only returns index 0, hiding additional members.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) then revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)); assumes single member but inherited grantRole allows direct multi-member creation

preconditions: Current OWNER calls grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, additionalAddress) directly
Multiple OWNER members now exist
Subsequent setOwner call removes wrong member

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No enforcement of single-OWNER invariant; owner() only reads index 0
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Current OWNER must call grantRole directly (intentional or accidental)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After direct grantRole creates additional OWNER members

operational_questions: Can getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) be monitored on-chain?
Does the owner() function mislead observers about actual OWNER count?
Is there an operational procedure to verify single-owner invariant?
validation_requirements: PoC: Owner A calls grantRole(OWNER, B), then setOwner(C), verify B retains OWNER
Verify owner() returns C while B still has upgrade authority
Test getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) after sequence

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or colluding OWNER calls grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, backDoorAddress)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: OWNER calls setOwner(legitimateNewOwner) as normal transfer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: setOwner revokes index 0 (original owner), but backDoorAddress persists at a higher index
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: backDoorAddress retains full OWNER privileges including upgrade authority, invisible to owner()
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires current OWNER to create backdoor

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Hidden OWNER retains full upgrade and role management authority
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol appears to have new legitimate owner while backdoor persists
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must detect hidden member via getRoleMemberCount and manually revoke

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: RoleGranted events for DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE outside setOwner calls
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct grantRole calls for OWNER role

threats.title: Shared Nonce Between permit() and transferWithAuthorization() Enables Cross-Function Signature Invalidation
threats.hypothesis_description: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping. Executing one function for a given address increments the nonce, permanently invalidating any pending signed message for the other function at the same nonce value. An attacker holding a valid transferWithAuthorization signature (from a prior interaction) can execute it to consume the user's nonce, invalidating a concurrently submitted permit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Both functions call _useNonce(owner/holder) from the same _nonces mapping at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol. Standard EIP-2612 and EIP-3009 use separate nonce systems to prevent this interaction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _useNonce(owner) called by both permit (line 55) and transferWithAuthorization (line 75); same _nonces mapping

preconditions: User has signed both a permit and a transferWithAuthorization at the same nonce
Attacker has access to one of the valid signatures
User's nonce has not advanced past the signed value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single nonce counter shared across two functionally distinct authorization types
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever a user has outstanding signatures for both functions

operational_questions: Do any integrations rely on both permit and TWA for the same user?
How are nonces managed by off-chain signing applications?
Would separate nonce namespaces resolve this?
validation_requirements: Sign permit at nonce N and TWA at nonce N for same user
Execute one, verify the other reverts with signature mismatch
Confirm nonce increment invalidates cross-function signature

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain a valid TWA signature from prior user interaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for user to sign a permit at the same nonce value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute the TWA to consume the nonce
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User's permit becomes invalid; must re-sign with new nonce
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires prior valid signature from user

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User authorization operations disrupted; must re-sign

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When user has pending signatures at same nonce

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rapid nonce consumption alternating between permit and TWA
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: TWA executions immediately before permit submissions

threats.title: Admin Bypasses Sender Blacklist and Pause Checks on Standard Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: adminSanity() exempts ADMIN from both pause and sender blacklist checks. While intended for forceTransfer, this exemption also applies to standard transfer() and transferFrom() when called by an ADMIN. A compromised ADMIN who is subsequently blacklisted can still transfer their own tokens out via regular transfer(), defeating the blacklist's containment purpose. The pause check bypass also means an ADMIN can move tokens during emergency pauses through regular transfer paths.
threats.investigation_rationale: adminSanity at ERC20AdminUpgradeable.sol:62-67 wraps pause and sender blacklist checks inside `if (!hasRole(ADMIN, _msgSender()))`. This bypass propagates to transfer/transferFrom via transferSanity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (!hasRole(ADMIN, _msgSender()))` skips both paused() and isBlacklisted(from) checks for ADMIN callers

preconditions: ADMIN address is compromised
Owner blacklists the ADMIN address
Owner pauses the contract
ADMIN role not yet revoked

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ADMIN exemption from blacklist/pause applies to standard transfers, not just forceTransfer
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Compromised ADMIN exploits their own privilege exemption
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between blacklisting/pausing and revoking ADMIN role

operational_questions: Is there an atomic operation to blacklist + revoke ADMIN simultaneously?
How quickly can OWNER respond to revoke ADMIN after detecting compromise?
Should adminSanity only bypass for forceTransfer, not standard transfers?
validation_requirements: Blacklist ADMIN address, have ADMIN call transfer(), verify success
Pause contract, have ADMIN call transfer(), verify success
Compare to non-ADMIN blacklisted address transfer behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised ADMIN is detected and blacklisted
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Before ADMIN role is revoked, ADMIN calls transfer() to move tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Blacklist check bypassed; transfer succeeds despite blacklisting
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: ADMIN extracts value before incident response completes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Compromised ADMIN can move funds during emergency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must revoke ADMIN role to contain; blacklist alone insufficient
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All transfer paths when caller has ADMIN role

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token movement during pause from ADMIN address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: ADMIN sending tokens immediately after blacklisting

threats.title: payGaslessBasefee Bypasses All Compliance Checks Enabling Fee Extraction From Blacklisted or Paused Accounts
threats.hypothesis_description: payGaslessBasefee() only verifies isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender) and payer balance, then calls _update(payer, paymaster, _gaslessBasefee) directly without adminSanity checks. It does not verify pause state, payer blacklist status, or paymaster blacklist status. A malicious contract set as trustedForwarder (via compromised ADMIN) could repeatedly call payGaslessBasefee to drain tokens from any address at _gaslessBasefee per call, bypassing all compliance controls.
threats.investigation_rationale: payGaslessBasefee at Token.sol:141-145 performs no adminSanity call. The _update call directly modifies balances without pause/blacklist verification, unlike all other token movement paths.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payGaslessBasefee checks only isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender) and balance; no adminSanity, no paused(), no isBlacklisted() checks

preconditions: A malicious address is set as trustedForwarder (requires ADMIN compromise)
OR legitimate Forwarder is exploited to call payGaslessBasefee independently
Target payer has balance >= _gaslessBasefee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: payGaslessBasefee has no compliance checks; unique among token movement functions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires ADMIN to set malicious trustedForwarder, or trustedForwarder contract to be compromised

operational_questions: Can the legitimate Forwarder be used to trigger payGaslessBasefee without a prior transfer?
Should payGaslessBasefee enforce blacklist and pause checks?
What is the maximum _gaslessBasefee that could be set?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious trustedForwarder, call payGaslessBasefee on paused contract, verify success
Call payGaslessBasefee with blacklisted payer, verify success
Verify legitimate Forwarder always pairs payGaslessBasefee with successful transfer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised ADMIN sets malicious contract as trustedForwarder
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Malicious forwarder calls payGaslessBasefee(victim, attacker) repeatedly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each call drains _gaslessBasefee from victim to attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bypasses all blacklist and pause protections
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires ADMIN compromise to set malicious forwarder

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Compliance controls completely bypassed for gasless fee path
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Blacklisted addresses can receive tokens; paused contract still moves value
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin resets trustedForwarder; lost tokens require forceTransfer recovery

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: payGaslessBasefee calls from unexpected addresses or during pause
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated payGaslessBasefee calls targeting same payer
